revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大天狗]][[zh:大天狗]]
{{Infobox deity|image=File:KuniyoshiUshiwakaTengu.jpg}}

'''Daitengu''' (大天狗, おおてんぐ、だいてんぐ) is a type of {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|qid=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}} reputed to possess powerful {{ill|Psychic|en|Psychic|qid=Q2917466|lt=spiritual power|1=Psychic}}.
Various theories exist regarding its nature; it is considered either a yokai (supernatural creature) or a deity with both benevolent and malevolent aspects. It is said that exceptionally powerful {{ill|Buddhist priest|ja|仏僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist priest|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=Sangha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q193944}}s and {{ill|ascetic|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ascetic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=Yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2309838}} sometimes transform into daitengu after death—consequently, they are believed to wield power far greater than that of other tengu.
== Appearance of Daitengu ==
In depictions of daitengu, the most common images include the “{{ill|Long-nosed Tengu|ja|鼻高天狗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-nosed Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|1=Daitengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10934709}},” which have noses longer than those of other tengu, and the “{{ill|Crow Tengu|ja|烏天狗|1=karasu tengu|2=ja|3=烏天狗|4=de|5=Karasu Tengu|6=ko|7=가라스텐구|8=es|9=Karasu Tengu|10=sv|11=Karasutengu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crow Tengu|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasu Tengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q863869}},” which appear as half-human, half-bird beings with wings on their backs. Other portrayals include figures such as {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|qid=Q349287|lt=Emperor Sutoku|1=Emperor Sutoku}}—appearing in works like the ''{{ill|Taiheiki|en|Taiheiki|qid=Q1152189|lt=Taiheiki|1=Taiheiki}}''—who is depicted as a “golden kite,” and in some cases, where an oni endures severe ascetic practices (as with the case of the Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō), the daitengu is represented in the form of an oni. There are even instances where a daitengu appears completely indistinguishable from a human.
== Japan's Great Demon King ==
From the medieval period onward, tengu came to be called "Great Demon Kings." Believed to have fallen from the true path of the tengu, they were attributed with immortality and supernatural powers akin to those of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|qid=Q1207451|lt=immortals|1=Xian (Taoism)}}. They were also thought to challenge {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. In some accounts, tengu were regarded as enormous monsters capable of shaking entire nations. Even if a particular tengu was not mighty enough to upheave a nation, their favorite mischief was well known. As stated in ''{{ill|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|en|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|qid=Q11594911|lt=Akuyacho Monogatari|1=Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari}}'', tengu were reputed to cause fires ({{ill|fire|ja|火事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 火災|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 火災|1=Conflagration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q168983}}), twisters ({{ill|Dust devil|en|Dust devil|qid=Q490600|lt=twister|1=Dust devil}}), petty quarrels, sumo bouts ({{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=sumo|1=Sumo}}) followed by group wrestling ({{ill|sumo wrestling|ja|組討|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sumo wrestling|1=sumo wrestling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16279661}}), disputes over festival territories (as symbolized by the aerial marker of the Shirakawa float and the ensuing contest over {{ill|Territory (animal)|en|Territory (animal)|qid=Q917215|lt=nawabari|1=Territory (animal)}}), and even demonstrations such as the vigorous shaking of portable shrines ({{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿振り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mikoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) at the gate of temples like {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}. Particularly, the fires and ensuing commotions caused by tengu were greatly feared.
== Giant Tengu ==
When it comes to legends of giants, the tale of {{ill|Daidarabotchi|en|Daidarabotchi|qid=Q1157529|lt=Daidarabotchi|1=Daidarabotchi}} is well known. Across various regions, pools and swamps said to be the footprints of a giant—and myths attributing the creation of mountains to such a being—have given rise to local legends where tengu are identified with Daidarabotchi, resulting in accounts of giant tengu.
== The Great Tengu of Emperor Sutoku ==
Among tengu legends, the story of {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|qid=Q349287|lt=Emperor Sutoku|1=Emperor Sutoku}} is particularly famous. In military tales such as those found in ''{{ill|The Tale of Hōgen|en|The Tale of Hōgen|qid=Q3007721|lt=Hōgen Monogatari|1=The Tale of Hōgen}}'' and ''{{ill|Ugetsu Monogatari|en|Ugetsu Monogatari|qid=Q1660401|lt=Ugetsu Monogatari|1=Ugetsu Monogatari}}'', Emperor Sutoku is depicted as having become "the great demon of Japan, seizing the emperor and making the people the emperor" by biting off his own tongue, inscribing a curse on a Mahāyāna sutra with his blood, and casting it into the sea. After his death, he is said to have returned as a vengeful spirit and tengu, wreaking havoc in the human world (although, in poems composed by the Sutoku-in and in ''{{ill|Imakagami|en|Imakagami|qid=Q3018114|lt=Imakagami|1=Imakagami}}'', while there is sorrow, no deep malice is evident).
== The Daitengu of Yakushikozu ==
{{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=Yakushikozu|1=En no Gyōja}} is celebrated as the progenitor of Japan's {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} and the founder of mountain religious practices. His exploits are recorded early in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', and later in texts such as ''{{ill|Nihon Reiiki|ja|日本霊異記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Reiiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本国現報善悪霊異記|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本国現報善悪霊異記|1=Nihon Ryōiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1606838}}'' and ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1507085}}'', which detail the severity of his mountain ascetic practices and his unsurpassed supernatural abilities. After attaining magical powers, he, like many ascetics, took up residence in the mountains—eating wild fruits, wearing leaves as garments, and journeying across various peaks. At Mount Katsuragi, he is said to have served the mountain deity Ichigon-shuumei; at Ikoma in Kawachi, he subdued two oni; and in the midst of Ōmine, he is reported to have accompanied both former and later oni, even riding on clouds. His miraculous feats are recognized in national history; in the 11th year of the {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|qid=Q1195131|lt=Kansei era|1=Kansei}} (1799), he was posthumously honored with the title of "{{ill|Shinpen Daibosatsu|ja|神変大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Daibosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q198946}}," and his opening of the mountain pilgrimage route, the Mine-nyūdō, connecting {{ill|Yoshino|ja|吉野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino|1=Yoshino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15410122}} and {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|qid=Q6443450|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano region}}, has spawned many successors, further enshrining his legendary status.
== Those Who Become Daitengu ==
In the dialogue recorded in ''{{ill|Genpei Jōsuiki|en|Genpei Jōsuiki|qid=Q3020148|lt=Genpei Seisuiki|1=Genpei Jōsuiki}}'' between Emperor {{ill|Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ōkami|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1114013}}, it is stated: 
　
"Among the myriad wise scholars and artisans, those whose hearts are exceedingly void of virtue, upon death, transform into demons known as tengu. Their heads assume the form of a tengu, their bodies remain human yet sprout wings, and they possess supernatural abilities that enable them to foresee events a hundred years hence and to fly through the air like falcons. Being monks, they do not descend into hell, yet due to their lack of moral virtue, they are denied rebirth and instead fall into the demonic path of the tengu. Monks with unvirtuous hearts and arrogant scholars all become demons and are thus called tengu."
　
Furthermore, it is said that monks of superior wisdom become daitengu, while those of lesser wisdom become small tengu.
== Tengu's Feather Fan ==
Among tengu, it is said that daitengu—or those with especially great power—possess a feather fan ({{ill|feather fan|ja|団扇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/feather fan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to うちわ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to うちわ|1=uchiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1056134}}). This fan is itself believed to have potent supernatural abilities. It is said that one either uses one's own feathers or obtains them from one's followers to create this fan. With a single feather fan, one can perform feats such as flight, {{ill|Shukuchi|en|Shukuchi|qid=Q10924459|lt=Shukuchi|1=Shukuchi}} (instantaneous travel), manifesting duplicates ({{ill|Fenshen|en|Fenshen|qid=Q16767422|lt=doppelganger|1=Fenshen}}), transformation ({{ill|Shapeshifting|en|Shapeshifting|qid=Q1925011|lt=transformation|1=Shapeshifting}}), and control over wind, rain, and fire, as well as influence over human hearts and subdue adversaries. Moreover, simply holding the fan while seated is said to repel demons and evil spirits, and it can even be used as a weapon to strike against wicked beasts or birds.
== Daitengu and Long-nosed Tengu ==
The extant ''{{ill|Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu|ja|鞍馬大僧正坊図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', attributed to {{ill|Kanō Motonobu|en|Kanō Motonobu|qid=Q1078015|lt=Kanō Motonobu|1=Kanō Motonobu}} (though some attribute it to another artist) and housed at {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|qid=Q608668|lt=Kurama-dera|1=Kurama-dera}} in Kyoto, is traditionally considered the first imagined depiction of daitengu in Japan; however, as will be discussed later, its depiction is not necessarily the first of its kind. Until then, tengu were predominantly depicted in the form of {{ill|Crow Tengu|ja|烏天狗|1=karasu tengu|2=ja|3=烏天狗|4=de|5=Karasu Tengu|6=ko|7=가라스텐구|8=es|9=Karasu Tengu|10=sv|11=Karasutengu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crow Tengu|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasu Tengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q863869}}. The daitengu depicted in the ''Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu'' exhibits a distinct appearance from the crow tengu, representing an image of daitengu that stands apart from conventional tengu and has come to spread across various mountains.
Since the early modern period, it has become customary to refer to long-nosed tengu as daitengu and crow tengu as small tengu. However, tengu of the Iinawa lineage still often retain the traditional appearance of crow tengu or kite-like figures.
Moreover, even before the ''{{ill|Tengusōshi|ja|天狗草紙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengusōshi|1=Tengu Zoshi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52132828}}''—compiled in the 4th year of the {{ill|Einin|en|Einin|qid=Q1327307|lt=Einin era|1=Einin}} (1296) in the Mitsuidera volume—depictions of tengu with long noses appear. These early depictions, created by a learned monk from the area of {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Hieizan|1=Mount Hiei}}, were not referred to as daitengu. This is because when the ''Tengusōshi'' was written in the late Kamakura period, the long-nosed style had not yet become predominant.<ref>[https://hdl.handle.net/10069/5859 A Study on the Iconographic Transformation of Tengu in Classical Literature: From Crow Tengu to Long-nosed Tengu] – Takaya Katsumata, Bulletin of the Faculty of Education, Nagasaki University, 2005</ref> In the ''Tengusōshi'', the term "Tengu no Chōrō" (elder tengu) refers to {{ill|Ippen Shōnin|ja|一遍上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ippen Shōnin|1=Ippen Shōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353222}}, who was reputed to have caused flowers to rain from the sky, trailed purple clouds, induced frenzied dancing among the people, and was even celebrated for his miraculous curative powers using urine—hence earning the epithet "elder tengu." This designation was partly a response, from other religious sects, to the overwhelming popularity of Ippen's teachings among the common people.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の歴史5 北朝と南朝|publisher=読売新聞社|year=1959|page=246|qq=}}</ref>
== Eight Tengu ==
The eight principal tengu in Japan are:
* {{ill|Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja|愛宕山太郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|1=Daitengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10934709}}
* {{ill|Hirayama Jirōbō|ja|比良山次郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirayama Jirōbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (referred to as “Jirōbō” in the ''{{ill|Tengukyō|ja|天狗経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengukyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'')
* {{ill|Iinawa Gongen|ja|飯綱三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iinawa Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|1=Izuna Gongen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666654}}
* {{ill|Kurama Tengu|ja|鞍馬山僧正坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|1=Sōjōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1786770}}
* {{ill|Ōyama Hakki-bō|ja|大山伯耆坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama Hakki-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (from the overview of {{ill|大山|en|大山|qid=Q1307003|lt=Ōyama (Kanagawa)|1=大山}})
* {{ill|Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja|彦山豊前坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as recounted in anecdotes from {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|qid=Q3077886|lt=Hikoyama|1=Mount Hiko}})
* {{ill|Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja|那智滝本前鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja|白峯相模坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
Furthermore, a separately distinguished tengu, {{ill|Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja|石鎚山法起坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is sometimes added.<ref>{{Cite video|和書|people=Go-shu (著者)|date=2016-06-29|title=The Country of Apparitions|publisher=ゴマブックス株式会社|medium=電子書籍|language=日本語|qq=}}</ref>
== Forty-eight Tengu ==
Among tengu, the Forty-eight Tengu described in the esoteric prayer sutra of the mid-Edo period, ''{{ill|Tengukyō|ja|天狗経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengukyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', are especially famous. They are listed as follows:
{{Columns-start|num=3|qq=}}
* {{ill|Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja|愛宕山太郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|1=Daitengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10934709}}
* {{ill|Hirayama Jirōbō|ja|比良山治郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirayama Jirōbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kurama Tengu|ja|鞍馬山僧正坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|1=Sōjōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1786770}}
* {{ill|Hieizan Hōsei-bō|ja|比叡山法性坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hieizan Hōsei-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Yokokawa Kakukai-bō|ja|横川覚海坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokokawa Kakukai-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Fujisan Darani-bō|ja|富士山陀羅尼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisan Darani-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nikkōsan Tōkō-bō|ja|日光山東光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkōsan Tōkō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Haguroyama Kinkō-bō|ja|羽黒山金光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haguroyama Kinkō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Myōgisan Nikko-bō|ja|妙義山日光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōgisan Nikko-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hitachi Tsukuba Hōin|ja|常陸筑波法印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Tsukuba Hōin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (linked as "徳一")
* {{ill|Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja|彦山豊前坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ōhara Sumiyoshi Kenbō|ja|大原住吉剣坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhara Sumiyoshi Kenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Echū Tateyama Nawatari-bō|ja|越中立山繩垂坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Tateyama Nawatari-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ama-no-Iwafune Dantoku-bō|ja|天岩船檀特坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama-no-Iwafune Dantoku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nara Ōkusugisaka-bō|ja|奈良大久杉坂坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Ōkusugisaka-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kumano Ōmine Kikujō-bō|ja|熊野大峯菊丈坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ōmine Kikujō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Columns-break|qq=}}
* {{ill|Yoshino Kanasugi Kosakurabō|ja|吉野皆杉小桜坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Kanasugi Kosakurabō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja|那智滝本前鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kōyasan Kōrin-bō|ja|高野山高林坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyasan Kōrin-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shintenyama Satoku-bō|ja|新田山佐徳坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintenyama Satoku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kikaijima Garan-bō|ja|鬼界島伽藍坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikaijima Garan-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Itadoyama Tondon-bō|ja|板遠山頓鈍坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itadoyama Tondon-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Saifu Takagaki Kōrin-bō|ja|宰府高垣高林坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifu Takagaki Kōrin-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nagato Fumō Kiyaku-bō|ja|長門普明鬼宿坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Fumō Kiyaku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Tsudoki Okin Fugen-bō|ja|都度沖普賢坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudoki Okin Fugen-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kimpira Gongen|ja|黒眷属金比羅坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimpira Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hyuga Obata Shinzō-bō|ja|日向尾畑新蔵坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyuga Obata Shinzō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Iōjima Kōtoku-bō|ja|醫王島光徳坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōjima Kōtoku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shikōzan Rikyu-bō|ja|紫黄山利久坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikōzan Rikyu-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hōki Ōyama Kiyomitsu-bō|ja|伯耆大山清光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Ōyama Kiyomitsu-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja|石鎚山法起坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Columns-break|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tenmansan Sanman-bō|ja|天満山三萬坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmansan Sanman-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Itsukushima Sankibō|ja|厳島三鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Sankibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shiraga-yama Kōseki-bō|ja|白髪山高積坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraga-yama Kōseki-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Akibayama Sanshaku-bō|ja|秋葉山三尺坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akibayama Sanshaku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋葉山本宮秋葉神社|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋葉山本宮秋葉神社|1=Akihasan Hongū Akiha Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q500413}}
* {{ill|Takao Naikuyō|ja|高雄内供奉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takao Naikuyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Iinawa Gongen|ja|飯綱三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iinawa Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|1=Izuna Gongen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666654}}
* {{ill|Ueno Myōgi-bō|ja|上野妙義坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueno Myōgi-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Higo Ajari|ja|肥後阿闍梨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Ajari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (linked as "皇円")
* {{ill|Katsuragi Kōten-bō|ja|葛城高天坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragi Kōten-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja|白峯相模坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kōryōsan Chikugo-bō|ja|高良山筑後坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryōsan Chikugo-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shōtōzan Kongō-bō|ja|象頭山金剛坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtōzan Kongō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kasagiyama Daisōjō|ja|笠置山大僧正|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasagiyama Daisōjō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Myōkōsan Adachi-bō|ja|妙高山足立坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkōsan Adachi-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Odaigatake Rokuseki-bō|ja|御嶽山六石坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaigatake Rokuseki-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Asama-ga-take Kanpei-bō|ja|浅間ヶ嶽金平坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asama-ga-take Kanpei-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Columns-end|qq=}}
When the suffix “–bō” is attached, it might be mistakenly taken as the individual name of a tengu; however, it is actually a name given to the mountain, and is better understood as the regional collective name for tengu. Essentially, the Forty-eight Tengu represent both “the dwelling place of the daitengu” and “the collective name for the daitengu and their retinues.” For example, in the case of {{ill|Itsukushima Sankibō|ja|厳島三鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Sankibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the mountain they inhabit is “Itsukushima,” the tengu group is called “Sankibō,” and the leading daitengu are understood to be Chūchō Kijin, Marakijin, and Tokumegikijin.
A thumbnail image is shown below:
[[File:KuniyoshiUshiwakaTengu.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|qid=Q227554|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|1=Ill}}’s print “Ushiwaramaru Sōjōbō Suibujutsu Kakuzu” (Kaei 4, 1851). At {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|qid=Q410132|lt=Kurama-dera|1=Mount Kurama}}, the daitengu {{ill|Kurama Tengu|ja|鞍馬山僧正坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|1=Sōjōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1786770}} (center) is depicted giving martial arts instruction to {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qid=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} (Ushiwaramaru, top right).]]
== Those Who Become Daitengu ==
In the ''{{ill|Genpei Jōsuiki|en|Genpei Jōsuiki|qid=Q3020148|lt=Genpei Seisuiki|1=Genpei Jōsuiki}}'', during a dialogue between Emperor {{ill|Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ōkami|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1114013}}, it is stated:
　
"Among the myriad wise scholars and skilled artisans, those whose hearts are exceedingly devoid of virtue, upon death, transform into demons called tengu. Their heads become that of a tengu, their bodies remain human yet sprout wings, and they possess supernatural abilities that enable them to foresee events a hundred years in advance and to fly through the air like falcons. Although being monks prevents them from falling into hell, their lack of moral virtue also precludes their attaining rebirth, and thus they fall into the demonic path of tengu. Monks with unvirtuous hearts and arrogant scholars all become demons, and are hence called tengu."
　
Furthermore, it is said that monks endowed with great wisdom become daitengu, while those with lesser wisdom become small tengu.
== Tengu's Feather Fan ==
Among tengu, it is said that the daitengu—or those of exceptional power—possess a feather fan ({{ill|feather fan|ja|団扇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/feather fan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to うちわ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to うちわ|1=uchiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1056134}}). This feather fan is itself believed to be imbued with potent supernatural abilities. It is said that one may fashion such a fan from one’s own feathers or those contributed by one’s dependents. With a single feather fan, one can freely execute feats such as flight, {{ill|Shukuchi|en|Shukuchi|qid=Q10924459|lt=Shukuchi|1=Shukuchi}} (instantaneous travel), duplication ({{ill|Fenshen|en|Fenshen|qid=Q16767422|lt=doppelganger|1=Fenshen}}), transformation ({{ill|Shapeshifting|en|Shapeshifting|qid=Q1925011|lt=transformation|1=Shapeshifting}}), and control over wind, rain, and fire, as well as influence human hearts and subdue adversaries. Moreover, merely holding the fan while seated is said to repel demons and evil spirits, and in some cases it is used as a weapon to strike against wicked beasts or birds.
== Daitengu and Long-nosed Tengu ==
The extant ''{{ill|Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu|ja|鞍馬大僧正坊図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', attributed to {{ill|Kanō Motonobu|en|Kanō Motonobu|qid=Q1078015|lt=Kanō Motonobu|1=Kanō Motonobu}} (though some claim it was created by another artist) and housed at {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|qid=Q608668|lt=Kurama-dera|1=Kurama-dera}} in Kyoto, is traditionally considered the first imagined depiction of daitengu in Japan; however, as will be discussed later, its form is not necessarily the original. Up until that time, tengu were predominantly depicted in the form of {{ill|Crow Tengu|ja|烏天狗|1=karasu tengu|2=ja|3=烏天狗|4=de|5=Karasu Tengu|6=ko|7=가라스텐구|8=es|9=Karasu Tengu|10=sv|11=Karasutengu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crow Tengu|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasu Tengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q863869}}. The daitengu depicted in the ''Kurama Daisōjōbō-zu'' exhibits a distinct appearance from the crow tengu, representing an image of daitengu that is a step above conventional tengu, and this image has spread throughout the mountains.
Since the early modern period, it has become common to refer to the long-nosed tengu as daitengu and the bird-like tengu as small tengu. However, tengu of the Iinawa lineage still often retain the traditional appearance of crow tengu or kite-like forms.
Furthermore, even before the ''{{ill|Tengusōshi|ja|天狗草紙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengusōshi|1=Tengu Zoshi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52132828}}'' (compiled in the 4th year of the {{ill|Einin|en|Einin|qid=Q1327307|lt=Einin era|1=Einin}}, i.e. 1296) in the Mitsuidera volume, depictions of tengu with long noses already existed. These early depictions, produced by a learned monk from the area of {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Hieizan|1=Mount Hiei}}, were not referred to as daitengu. This is because when the ''Tengusōshi'' was written in the late Kamakura period, the long-nosed style had not yet become predominant.<ref>[https://hdl.handle.net/10069/5859 A Study on the Iconographic Transformation of Tengu in Classical Literature: From Crow Tengu to Long-nosed Tengu] – Takaya Katsumata, Bulletin of the Faculty of Education, Nagasaki University, 2005</ref> In the ''Tengusōshi'', the term "Tengu no Chōrō" (elder tengu) is used to refer to {{ill|Ippen Shōnin|ja|一遍上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ippen Shōnin|1=Ippen Shōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353222}}, who was said to have performed miraculous feats such as causing flowers to rain from the sky, trailing purple clouds, and inciting wild dancing among the people—even being venerated as a miraculous healer using urine. This appellation of "elder tengu" was partly a reaction from other sects against the widespread popular enthusiasm for Ippen's teachings among the common people.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の歴史5 北朝と南朝|publisher=読売新聞社|year=1959|page=246|qq=}}</ref>
== Eight Tengu ==
Japan’s representative eight tengu are:
* {{ill|Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja|愛宕山太郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|1=Daitengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10934709}}
* {{ill|Hirayama Jirōbō|ja|比良山次郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirayama Jirōbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (referred to as “Jirōbō” in the ''{{ill|Tengukyō|ja|天狗経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengukyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'')
* {{ill|Iinawa Gongen|ja|飯綱三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iinawa Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|1=Izuna Gongen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666654}}
* {{ill|Kurama Tengu|ja|鞍馬山僧正坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|1=Sōjōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1786770}}
* {{ill|Ōyama Hakki-bō|ja|大山伯耆坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama Hakki-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja|彦山豊前坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja|那智滝本前鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nachi Taki Motomae Oni-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja|白峯相模坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
Furthermore, the separately distinguished {{ill|Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja|石鎚山法起坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san Hōkibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is sometimes added.<ref>{{Cite video|和書|people=Go-shu (著者)|date=2016-06-29|title=The Country of Apparitions|publisher=ゴマブックス株式会社|medium=電子書籍|language=日本語|qq=}}</ref>
== Forty-eight Tengu ==
Among tengu, the Forty-eight Tengu described in the esoteric prayer sutra of the mid-Edo period, ''{{ill|Tengukyō|ja|天狗経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengukyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', are especially famous. They are listed as follows:
{{Columns-start|num=3|qq=}}
* {{ill|Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja|愛宕山太郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atago Yama Tarōbō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大天狗|1=Daitengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10934709}}
* {{ill|Hirayama Jirōbō|ja|比良山治郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirayama Jirōbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kurama Tengu|ja|鞍馬山僧正坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurama Tengu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鞍馬天狗|1=Sōjōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1786770}}
* {{ill|Hieizan Hōsei-bō|ja|比叡山法性坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hieizan Hōsei-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Yokokawa Kakukai-bō|ja|横川覚海坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokokawa Kakukai-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Fujisan Darani-bō|ja|富士山陀羅尼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisan Darani-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nikkōsan Tōkō-bō|ja|日光山東光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkōsan Tōkō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Haguroyama Kinkō-bō|ja|羽黒山金光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haguroyama Kinkō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Myōgisan Nikko-bō|ja|妙義山日光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōgisan Nikko-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hitachi Tsukuba Hōin|ja|常陸筑波法印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Tsukuba Hōin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja|彦山豊前坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoyama Hōzenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ōhara Sumiyoshi Kenbō|ja|大原住吉剣坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhara Sumiyoshi Kenbō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Echū Tateyama Nawatari-bō|ja|越中立山繩垂坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Tateyama Nawatari-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ama-no-Iwafune Dantoku-bō|ja|天岩船檀特坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama-no-Iwafune Dantoku-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Nara Ōkusugisaka-bō|ja|奈良大久杉坂坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Ōkusugisaka-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kumano Ōmine Kikujō-bō|ja|熊野大峯菊丈坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ōmine Kikujō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Columns-break|qq=}}

* {{ill|Itadoyama Tondon-bō|ja|板遠山頓鈍坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itadoyama Tondon-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

{{Columns-break|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tenmansan Sanman-bō|ja|天満山三萬坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmansan Sanman-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

* {{ill|Takao Naikuyō|ja|高雄内供奉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takao Naikuyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Iinawa Gongen|ja|飯綱三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iinawa Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飯縄権現|1=Izuna Gongen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666654}}
* {{ill|Ueno Myōgi-bō|ja|上野妙義坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueno Myōgi-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Higo Ajari|ja|肥後阿闍梨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Ajari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Katsuragi Kōten-bō|ja|葛城高天坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragi Kōten-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja|白峯相模坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahone Sagami-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kōryōsan Chikugo-bō|ja|高良山筑後坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryōsan Chikugo-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Shōtōzan Kongō-bō|ja|象頭山金剛坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtōzan Kongō-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kasagiyama Daisōjō|ja|笠置山大僧正|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasagiyama Daisōjō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Myōkōsan Adachi-bō|ja|妙高山足立坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkōsan Adachi-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Odaigatake Rokuseki-bō|ja|御嶽山六石坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaigatake Rokuseki-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Asama-ga-take Kanpei-bō|ja|浅間ヶ嶽金平坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asama-ga-take Kanpei-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Columns-end|qq=}}
Note that the suffix “–bō” is not to be taken as an individual name but rather as the name assigned to a mountain; it is better understood as the collective regional name for a group of tengu. Essentially, the Forty-eight Tengu denote both “the dwelling place of the daitengu” and “the collective name for the daitengu and their retinues.” For example, in the case of {{ill|Itsukushima Sankibō|ja|厳島三鬼坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Sankibō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the mountain of residence is “Itsukushima,” the tengu group is called “Sankibō,” and the leading daitengu are understood to be Chūchō Kijin, Marakijin, and Tokumegikijin.
